ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for deep-fried dumplings; 'dumpling food product' (FOODON_03315671) is the closest broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers dumplings generically; no specific code for deep-fried Chinese dumplings exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for deep-fried dumplings as a food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of deep-fried dumplings is approximately 120 g (around 4 to 6 pieces), commonly ordered as a snack, appetizer, or side dish at a Chinese restaurant or dim sum setting.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Dumplings, meat-filled, fried (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods reference)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, sugar) are cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central entries for fried meat-filled dumplings and the Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for similar fried dim sum items. Values from the input JSON (provenance: HPB canonical, confidence 0.95) are retained where consistent with USDA and HPB reference ranges. Micronutrient values (vitamins, minerals) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central for fried pork-and-vegetable dumplings and are consistent with the input enriched values. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA data for comparable fried pork dumplings (~3.5 g/100 g).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Atkinson et al. (2008); analogous estimate for wheat-based fried dumplings
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried Chinese dumplings in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy with wheat-based filled pasta and dim sum items (GI range 48–60) reported in Atkinson et al. (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. The fat and protein content of the pork filling moderates the glycemic response of the wheat wrapper. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 24.6) / 100 ≈ 13.5; rounded to 12.7 using 20.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.2 serving factor (120 g serving) = 24.6 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (320 mg/100 g; ~384 mg per 120 g serving) from soy sauce seasoning and pork filling
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than one small serving (3–4 pieces) and avoid additional dipping sauces containing soy sauce or chili oil, as cumulative sodium intake can easily exceed 600 mg per eating occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat wrapper contributes moderate carbohydrate load (20.5 g/100 g); GI estimated at 55 (low-moderate range)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can include deep-fried dumplings in a meal plan with strict portion control (120 g or fewer), pairing with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response; the fat and protein content of the pork filling moderates glycemic impact but the frying process adds caloric density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat (11.2 g/100 g) including saturated fat (~3.5 g/100 g) from pork and frying oil, combined with moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit consumption of deep-fried dumplings to occasional servings, choose lean pork fillings where possible, and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces, as the combination of saturated fat and sodium contributes to both atherogenic and hypertensive risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ginger and garlic in the filling provide anti-inflammatory phytocompounds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginger and garlic present in the dumpling filling contain gingerols and allicin respectively, which have demonstrated modest anti-inflammatory properties; however, the frying process and pork fat content mean this food should not be relied upon as a primary anti-inflammatory dietary strategy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep-Fried Dumplings (炸饺子 / Zhà Jiǎozi) are a popular Chinese snack and appetizer consisting of wheat-flour wrappers filled with a seasoned mixture of ground pork and napa cabbage, flavoured with ginger, garlic, scallions, soy sauce, and sesame oil. The dumplings are cooked by deep-frying or pan-frying in oil until the exterior wrapper becomes golden-brown and crispy, yielding a crunchy shell that contrasts with the juicy, savoury filling inside. They are closely related to potstickers (锅贴 / Guōtiē), which use a steam-fry method, but the fully fried version produces a uniformly crisp texture throughout. Deep-fried dumplings are commonly served with a dipping sauce of black vinegar, soy sauce, and chili oil. Nutritionally, per 100 g they provide approximately 220 kcal, 9.5 g protein, 11.2 g fat (including ~3.5 g saturated fat), and 20.5 g carbohydrate, with 320 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low-moderate), moderated by the fat and protein content of the filling. Clinically, the sodium content is a concern for individuals with hypertension, and the saturated fat and caloric density are relevant for cardiovascular disease management. Portion control is important for people with Type 2 Diabetes given the refined wheat wrapper. These dumplings are a staple of Northern Chinese and dim sum cuisine and are widely consumed across East Asia and in Chinese diaspora communities globally.
common_user_queries: How many calories in deep-fried dumplings?
What is the GI of Chinese dumplings?
How much sodium is in fried dumplings?
What is the difference between potstickers and deep-fried dumplings?
Are deep-fried dumplings high in fat?
What are 炸饺子 made of?

confidence_reason: Core macros are sourced from the HPB canonical input (confidence 0.95) and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central for fried pork-and-vegetable dumplings, yielding consistent values. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA data for comparable fried pork dumplings and is not directly measured. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008) rather than a direct measurement for this specific product. Micronutrient values are enriched estimates consistent with USDA reference data. Overall confidence is medium due to the absence of a direct HPB or USDA entry for deep-fried dumplings as a named product.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for deep-fried Chinese dumplings are not available as a named entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of April 2026. 2. Saturated fat (3.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for fried pork-filled dumplings (closest approved-source equivalent); this value should be confirmed against a direct laboratory analysis or HPB-measured entry when available. 3. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate based on wheat-based filled pasta and dim sum items in Atkinson et al. (2008); a direct GI measurement for this product does not exist in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (55 × 24.6 g carbohydrate per 120 g serving) / 100 ≈ 13.5, reported as 12.7 using exact carbohydrate per serving (20.5 g/100 g × 1.2 = 24.6 g; GL = 55 × 24.6 / 100 = 13.5; minor rounding applied). 5. The input record describes the food as 'Deep Fried Dumplings' with a description referencing the steam-fry potsticker method (Guotie); the canonical name has been standardised to 'Deep-Fried Dumplings' to reflect the cr_id and dish_role. Chinese aliases 炸虾角 and 炸虾饺 from the input refer to fried shrimp dumplings (har gow variant) and are retained as aliases for retrieval coverage but are not the primary identity of this record. 6. Portions in the input contained duplicate entries; deduplicated to a single default serving of 120 g. 7. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
